contest.title: Neowise Labs Contest 1 (Codeforces Round 1018, Div. 1 + Div. 2)

sources.notes: Statement and constraints: repeatedly sort a chosen length-3 substring of a binary-colored sequence and minimize the number of operations needed to sort all black before pink.
Official tutorial confirms the four possible operation types, inversion reduction values, the parity correction using black bears on even positions, and the formula (inversions + correction) / 2.
Luogu solution cross-checks the same inversion-count lower bound and the correction term based on the target parity distribution of black bears.

annotation.summary: Given a binary sequence of teddy bear colors, one operation sorts any length-3 substring; compute the minimum number of operations needed to make all black bears precede all pink bears.
annotation.constraints: Sum n <= 200000; the solution must be linear or near-linear and cannot simulate swaps.
annotation.core_idea: Treat black as 0 and pink as 1, so the target is the sorted binary sequence. Sorting a length-3 substring has four meaningful forms: 010 and 101 reduce the inversion count by 1, while 100 and 110 reduce it by 2. Let x be the total number of inversions. We would like to use only the operations that reduce two inversions, but the final sorted sequence also imposes a parity-position condition on where the black bears end up. Let a be the total number of black bears and let b be the number of black bears currently on even positions. In the sorted sequence, the number of black bears on even positions is floor(a/2). Only the one-inversion operations can change this count, so at least d = |floor(a/2)-b| such operations are necessary. After those d operations, the remaining x-d inversions can be removed two at a time, giving answer d + (x-d)/2 = (x+d)/2.
tricks: Use inversion count as the main progress measure.
Classify all length-3 binary patterns by how many inversions sorting them removes.
Use parity-position imbalance to count how many one-inversion operations are unavoidable.

solution_variants.name: inversion-plus-parity-correction
solution_variants.summary: Scan the string to count inversions and black bears on even positions. Compute the target even-position black count in the sorted string, take the absolute difference d, and output (inversions + d) / 2.

tags.evidence: The answer is expressed in terms of the total number of pink-before-black inversions plus a correction term.
tags.description: Use the number of inversions or an inversion-like statistic as the main potential function, lower bound, or exact cost component.
tags.created_reason: Existing sorting/greedy tags do not identify problems where inversion count is the decisive potential function.

tags.evidence: The correction term counts how far the current even-position black count is from the sorted sequence's required even-position count.
The proof prioritizes operations removing two inversions and shows only the parity correction forces one-inversion operations.
The operation is local sorting of length-3 binary substrings and the target is the sorted binary sequence.
The even-position black count changes only under the one-inversion operation types, which creates the unavoidable correction.
